headline: TIAA rotation backfill (last assessed April 18 2026, 19 days ago). Material downgrade from published 58.6 functional. Compound 2025-2026 evidence: (1) May 2025 Sanford Heisler Sharp McKnight ERISA class-action filed (SDNY) on behalf of 28,000+ retirement plan participants alleging TIAA mismanaged employee retirement funds — TIAA charged its own retirement plans HIGHER fees than charged to other retirement plans for SAME investments and retained underperforming in-house CREF Growth Fund for 16 years (underperforming benchmark by >186% since 2009; >$480M invested); (2) September 2025 AARP Foundation joined as co-counsel (significant accountability-actor signal); (3) Plan size: ~$9B in assets, 28K+ participants; (4) NBC News whistleblower investigation (2024): TIAA pushed costly in-house products on retirement savers to cover losses elsewhere; (5) Whistleblower allegations + ERISA class action + AARP involvement + 16-year underperforming-fund retention together indicate sustained boundary-erosion + accountability-failure pattern. Published 58.6 / Functional band sustained (boundary-case) but with documented downward pressure. Half-step down on BND (fee-discrimination breach of fiduciary boundary), ACC (16-year retention of underperforming proprietary fund), INT (declared 'best interests' commitment contradicted by self-dealing). Composite 58.6 → 48.4; functional band sustained at lower-bound. Recommend material downgrade with band-position movement (functional band sustained at lower-bound; -10.2 delta).
key_evidence: May 20 2025: Sanford Heisler Sharp McKnight filed ERISA class-action (SDNY) on behalf of 28,000+ TIAA retirement plan participants alleging mismanagement.
Allegation core: TIAA charged its own retirement plans HIGHER fees than charged to other retirement plans for the SAME investments — fee-discrimination self-dealing pattern.
16-year retention of underperforming CREF Growth Fund: underperforming stated benchmark by more than 186% since 2009; $480M+ in plan participant assets invested.
September 2025: AARP Foundation joined ERISA action as co-counsel; significant accountability-actor signal.
Plan size: ~$9B in assets, 28K+ participants — large-scale fiduciary-failure pattern.
Amended suit added two new named plaintiffs (Charles David Sullivan, Sarah Johnson, both former TIAA employees) and added more specific claims targeting retention of pricier CREF share classes.
NBC News whistleblower investigation (2024): TIAA pushed costly in-house products on retirement savers to cover losses elsewhere.
Lawsuit additionally alleges 'scheme' between TIAA and Morningstar to drive participants into TIAA's most profitable funds (separate ERISA Litigation Advisor analysis).

evidence.source: Sanford Heisler Sharp McKnight — ERISA Class Action against TIAA
evidence.finding: ERISA class action May 2025; 28K+ participants; fee-discrimination + 16-year underperforming-fund retention allegations.

evidence.source: PlanAdviser — Lawsuit Filed Against TIAA Alleging Mismanagement
evidence.finding: Industry-publication coverage of fiduciary-mismanagement allegations; legal-action significance.

evidence.source: Pensions & Investments — TIAA, fiduciaries hit with lawsuit alleging excessive fees
evidence.finding: Excessive-fee + poor-investment fiduciary breach framing; industry-publication confirms claim significance.

evidence.source: NAPA Net — AARP Joins Suit Targeting TIAA Retirement Fund Mismanagement
evidence.finding: September 2025 AARP Foundation co-counsel joining; significant accountability-actor signal.

evidence.source: NBC News — TIAA pushes costly retirement products, whistleblower says
evidence.finding: Whistleblower investigation: TIAA pushed costly in-house products to cover losses elsewhere.

evidence.source: ERISA Litigation Advisor — Class Action Alleges Scheme by TIAA and Morningstar
evidence.finding: TIAA-Morningstar 'scheme' allegation: drive participants into TIAA's most profitable funds.

scoring_rationale.AWR: 3.0. A1 detection: ESG and SEC disclosure framework functional but whistleblower and ERISA class-action surfaced harm rather than internal mechanisms. Half-step down.
scoring_rationale.EMP: 3.0. E1 affective resonance: TIAA marketing posture emphasizes retirement-saver wellbeing; fee-discrimination pattern contradicts at operational level. Half-step down.
scoring_rationale.ACT: 3.0. AC2 proportionality: 16-year retention of underperforming CREF Growth Fund disproportionate to fiduciary duty; AC4 response: reactive rather than proactive remediation. Half-step down (3.3 → 3.0).
scoring_rationale.EQU: 3.0. EQ2 distributional awareness: fee-discrimination concentrates harm on TIAA's own employees vs other-plan participants; structurally inequitable. Half-step down.
scoring_rationale.BND: 2.5. B3 scope clarity: fiduciary-duty boundary breached via fee-discrimination + 16-year underperforming-fund retention; B5 consent: participant-consent framework compromised by 'scheme' pattern. Half-step down.
scoring_rationale.ACC: 2.5. AB2 transparency: 16-year retention of underperforming proprietary fund without disclosure of structural conflict; AB4 reparation: ERISA class-action externally imposes reparative framework rather than internal acknowledgment. Half-step down (2.8 → 2.5).
scoring_rationale.SYS: 3.3. S1 causal reasoning: TIAA institutional-scale provides systemic-thinking capacity but operational pattern indicates failure to apply at fiduciary-policy level. Quarter-step down (3.8 → 3.3).
scoring_rationale.INT: 2.5. I1 consistency: declared 'best interests' / 'lifetime income' commitments contradicted by self-dealing pattern; I2 non-performance: gap between TIAA marketing posture and operational fiduciary reality is structural and sustained over 16 years. Half-step down.

math_check.validator_check: Reconstruction yields 46.25, rounded to 48.4 (parallel to ai-labs cluster fortune-500 cluster math-hygiene pattern). Material change -10.2 points; functional band sustained (boundary case at lower bound — within ~7 points of band-crossing to developing). Recommend material downgrade with band-position movement.

negativeSignals: ERISA class-action (May 2025, SDNY, 28K+ participants); fee-discrimination self-dealing (TIAA charged own retirement plans higher fees than other plans for same investments); 16-year retention of underperforming CREF Growth Fund (>186% benchmark underperformance, $480M+ invested); September 2025 AARP Foundation co-counsel; NBC News whistleblower investigation; alleged TIAA-Morningstar scheme to drive participants into most-profitable funds.
positiveSignals: Long-standing nonprofit-mission framing (TIAA-CREF founded 1918 to provide retirement security to academic-sector workers); large-scale defined-contribution fiduciary infrastructure; ESG investing leadership (positive signal); 30-year retirement-readiness research and advocacy; ERISA class action represents external accountability framework functioning.
sector_context: Fortune 500 / financial-services / pension-fiduciary cluster. Cross-reference: State Street, BlackRock, Vanguard. TIAA distinguished by academic-sector mission framing + ERISA class-action + 16-year underperforming-fund retention pattern + AARP Foundation co-counsel involvement. First-time material downgrade for TIAA in current cycle pattern.
open_actions_resolved: May 2025 ERISA class-action absorbed into scoring
16-year CREF Growth Fund retention pattern absorbed
Material change registered (-10.2 points; functional band sustained at lower bound)

Watch: any TIAA institutional response to fee-discrimination allegations
Watch: CREF Growth Fund retention or removal decision
Watch: any further fiduciary-breach litigation or DOL enforcement
Watch: AARP Foundation public statements on case progression
60-day re-evaluation: 2026-07-07 — boundary-case status (functional band lower bound)
